title: Boudin noir aux pommes
subtitle: pan-fried black pudding with butter-soft apple
excerpt: Sliced black pudding fried until the skin crisps, served alongside apple wedges cooked in butter until golden and yielding. This is quick bistro food at its most direct: ready in under twenty minutes, good enough for supper on its own with a green salad alongside.
sourceNotes:
J.-B. Reboul, La Cuisinière Provençale (1897). General charcuterie preparation methods and pan-frying techniques for boudin.

A. Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903). Boudin noir preparation and the classic pairing with sautéed apple, public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: A French black pudding made from pork blood and fat, seasoned with onion and spices, and stuffed into a casing. It is richer and more finely textured than British black pudding, though the two are closely related and interchangeable in this recipe.

content.text: Boudin noir aux pommes is one of the great simple suppers of the French bistro kitchen.

goes into a hot pan, the skin crisps and the inside becomes soft and yielding. The apple goes in alongside with plenty of butter, cooking until it turns golden and just starts to collapse. Together they are the kind of dish that looks unremarkable on a menu and tastes far better than it has any right to.

The cooking is almost entirely about heat management. Black pudding skin will burst if the pan is too fierce, and apple will turn to mush if you cook it too long. Get both right and the two things can share the pan at the end for a minute to warm through together. The whole process from cold pan to plate takes fifteen minutes.

items.prepNote: peeled, cored, cut into thick wedges

content.text: You need a large frying pan, wide enough to hold the black pudding slices in a single layer. A 30 cm pan handles four servings without crowding. If yours is smaller, cook in two batches and keep the first batch warm while you do the second.

Melt half the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the pan over medium heat. When it foams, add the {{apple-eating}} apple wedges in a single layer. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes per side until golden and just soft at the edges. If using the {{shallot}} shallots, add them with the apple and let them soften alongside. Remove and keep warm on a plate.

Wipe the pan and add the remaining butter over medium-high heat. When the butter foams and begins to colour, lay in the {{black-pudding}} of black pudding slices. Cook for 2 minutes without moving them, so the skin sets and crisps underneath. Turn carefully with a spatula and cook for 2 minutes more. The inside will be hot and soft; the casing will be lightly charred in places. Season with black pepper.

Return the apple to the pan alongside the black pudding for 30 seconds to heat through and pick up any buttery juices from the bottom. Serve immediately: the black pudding firms up as it cools. A simple green salad or a few leaves of watercress alongside is all you need.

items.symptom: Skin splits and the pudding falls apart in the pan
items.cause: Pan too hot or slices too thin
items.fix: Lower the heat to medium and handle the slices as little as possible; slices should be at least 1.5 cm thick so they hold together while cooking

items.symptom: Apple turns to mush before the black pudding is done
items.cause: Apple cooked at too high a heat, or cooked for too long
items.fix: Cook the apple at medium heat and remove it while it still has a little resistance; it will soften further in the residual heat on the plate

items.symptom: Inside of the black pudding is cold while the outside is coloured
items.cause: Pan too hot; the outside cooked before the inside warmed through
items.fix: Reduce the heat after the first turn and cover the pan loosely for 1 minute to let the heat penetrate

items.cause: No acidity to cut the richness of the black pudding
items.fix: Splash a little cider vinegar into the hot pan after the black pudding comes out, swirl, and spoon over the plate. A spoonful of Dijon on the side also helps

content.text: Boudin noir aux pommes is a staple of the Lyon bouchon, the worker's bistros where offal and charcuterie form the backbone of the menu. The pairing of dark, iron-rich black pudding with sweet cooked apple turns up across northern France and into the Basque country. It is an autumn dish: new-season puddings arrive with the apple harvest.
